context: domain.md adopted the spec guide's default 8-endpoint API surface and the default ListDetailPanel UI layout without deviations. For entity, the same defaults fit cleanly, but two entity-specific UI questions need explicit decisions: (a) whether to add a Domains column to the master pane (entity-to-domain affiliation is entity's defining classification in v0.4), and (b) what the create-dialog domain-affiliation flow looks like (create-then-attach via the detail pane vs create-with-attach via a multi-select in the dialog itself).
decision: Adopt standard 8-endpoint API surface with no deviations: GET /entities, GET/PUT/PATCH/DELETE /entities/{id}, POST /entities, POST /entities/{id}/restore, GET /entities/next-identifier. Server-side status-transition validation per the domain pattern. Only ?include_deleted=true list filter in v0.4. Decomposed reference handling: domain affiliations NOT inlined into entity create/update bodies; client makes separate POST /references calls. Default ListDetailPanel UI in the existing Methodology sidebar group (position #2 after Domains). Standard four-column master pane (identifier, name, status, updated) — defer a Domains column to v0.5+ paired with PI-007 short codes, tracked as new PI-009. Detail pane shows fields in section-3.2 order with entity_notes under a collapsible 'Internal notes' header collapsed by default (mirrors domain). Edge-text confirmation on delete. Create-dialog domain-affiliation flow (create-then-attach vs create-with-attach) left as an open question for the v0.4-build-planning conversation to decide. 16 testable acceptance criteria covering schema migration, identifier format constraint, name uniqueness, status enum/transition validation, access-layer methods, REST endpoint round-trip, identifier-auto-assignment concurrency, soft-delete/restore, sidebar entry, master pane columns, detail pane fields, CRUD dialogs, file-watch refresh, plus three specific to the new vocabulary registration and references mechanism.
rationale: No-deviation default endpoints minimize cross-spec complexity and reuse existing v2 patterns. Decomposed reference handling keeps the entity API consistent with v2's references-first discipline (DEC-006); shortcut endpoints would invent API surface area that the references-table API already covers. Domains column deferred to v0.5+ for two reasons: (a) without PI-007 short codes, the column would render DOM-001/002/003 — identifiers that mean nothing at a glance, so the column's UX value is tied to PI-007 landing; (b) the batched-join query design isn't free for a column whose value the detail pane already exposes one click away via ReferencesSection. Shipping the column alongside PI-007 keeps both deliverables aligned. Create-dialog flow left open because both candidate patterns satisfy the acceptance criterion that the user can attach affiliations without leaving the UI; the choice is UI-implementation, not schema-design, and the v0.4-build-planning conversation has the right cross-cutting visibility to decide.
title: entity API surface, UI defaults, deferred Domains-column posture, acceptance criteria for v0.4

alternatives_considered: PRC (three letters, reads as typo, rejected). PR (two letters, collides with public relations, rejected). P (one letter, too short, rejected). PROCESS (seven letters, verbose, breaks rhythm with other prefixes, rejected).
consequences: process.md uses PROC-NNN throughout. No collision with existing prefixes. The crm_candidate spec conversation retains the soft-3-letter posture's flexibility-budget and decides its own prefix in its own section 3.1 (working assumption from SES-011 workstream plan was CRM but is potentially ambiguous against product-name; may end up as CRMC or similar). Cross-spec consistency check at v0.4-build planning verifies no prefix collisions across the four methodology entity types.
context: The process methodology entity needs an identifier prefix. Per domain.md section 3.1 the soft-3-letter posture preserves flexibility for downstream methodology entity types to deviate to four letters when the three-letter form is ambiguous; the example given was PROC over PRC for clarity.
decision: Adopt PROC as the identifier prefix for the process methodology entity. Format PROC-NNN, zero-padded to 3 digits, server-assigned on POST omission via the GET /processes/next-identifier helper (per DEC-043 / SES-010 resolution).
rationale: PROC reads unambiguously as 'process' and avoids the genuine readability problem with PRC. The three-letter candidate PRC reads as a typo or an obscure acronym; consultants would have to mentally re-expand it every time. PR collides with public relations (which a CRM context might legitimately encounter as a domain or department) and is only two letters anyway. P is too short. PROC has no collision with existing prefixes (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT). The four-letter form invokes domain.md's explicit deviation clause; no precedent is broken.
title: process identifier prefix and format

alternatives_considered: Per-(domain, name) name uniqueness (rejected for downstream documentation rendering ambiguity). Two-content-field split mirroring domain (purpose + description, rejected because for process both questions collapse to one). Three-value status field mirroring domain/entity (rejected because the methodology's process lifecycle is definitional, not propose-verify, and v0.4 has only one definitional level). Conditional-required process_classification_rationale (rejected as schema complexity; optional with UI placeholder cuing is simpler).
consequences: process.md schema in v0.4 has eight substantive fields plus inherited timestamps. Cross-spec consistency check at v0.4-build planning verifies the deviation rationale is acceptable (it is — the spec guide section 6 permits deviation with explicit justification, and the deviation is explicitly justified). When v0.5+ introduces process schema growth (PI-005), a definitional-completeness lifecycle field returns. Future scalar implementation-priority is tracked separately as PI-011.
context: The process methodology entity must host the Phase 1 Prioritized Backbone output (section 7.3 of phase-1-interview-guide.md): per process, a client-language name, a one-sentence purpose, a domain affiliation, a priority classification, the reasoning behind the classification, and the handoffs to/from other backbone processes. Phase 1 explicitly does not produce detailed process definitions; that's Phase 3 territory deferred under PI-005. The minimum-viable v0.4 schema captures only what Phase 1 produces.
decision: Adopt a thin field inventory: process_identifier, process_name (required, engagement-global case-insensitive unique), process_domain_identifier (required, direct FK to domain.domain_identifier), process_purpose (required, one-sentence), process_classification_rationale (optional, classification reasoning text), process_notes (optional, internal scratchpad), process_classification (separate decision, see DEC-057), plus inherited timestamps. No separate process_description (one purpose field carries the work-content meaning for process the way two fields don't need to). No process_kind / base-type classification. No storage-level length caps. No process_status field — a deviation from domain.md / entity.md precedent — because the methodology's lifecycle on process is about definitional completeness, not propose-verify scope, and v0.4 schema has only the identified level (a status field with one value is no field at all). The engagement-scope information that domain and entity carry via status=deferred is carried for process by process_classification=deferred instead.
rationale: Engagement-global name uniqueness was initially proposed as per-(domain, name) unique to allow natural client-language names like 'Recruit' in both Mentor Recruitment and Client Recruiting domains. The user pushed back applying the principle 'if there could ever be user confusion, then we should go engagement specific' — process handoff lists in Phase 1 output documents reference process names alone without domain context, creating ambiguity risk under per-domain uniqueness. Engagement-global mirrors precedent and matches CBM's existing pattern of suffixing names (Mentor Recruit, Client Recruit). The no-status-field deviation rationale: domain and entity use status to track propose-verify engagement scope; process has process_classification serving exactly that role (a process with classification=deferred is acknowledged-but-out-of-current-scope, same semantic as status=deferred for domain/entity). Adding a separate status field would either duplicate the meaning or introduce field-collision ambiguity (which 'deferred' applies?). A definitional-completeness lifecycle field (working name process_definition_level) returns in v0.5+ when PI-005 introduces detailed-process schema content with multiple completeness levels distinguishable in the schema. Single-content-field choice (purpose, no separate description) follows the same reasoning entity.md applied — for process, both questions reduce to 'what does this process do?' and one field handles it without information loss.
title: process field inventory under minimum-viable v0.4 scope, with no-status-field deviation and engagement-global name uniqueness

alternatives_considered: process_priority as field name (rejected — semantically misleading per user feedback). process_priority with values Critical/High/Medium/Low replacing the three-bucket classification (rejected — would diverge from Phase 1 7.3 output specification which explicitly produces Backbone / Supporting / Deferred sections by name; methodology change should precede schema change). process_tier as field name (acceptable alternative but classification is closer to the methodology's actual term). Required-no-default classification (rejected — incompatible with Phase 1 Session 1 capture cadence). Free regression to unclassified after classification (rejected — same rationale as domain's one-way propose-verify gate: post-decision state is meaningful and should be re-expressed via the classified values rather than abandoned).
consequences: process records start unclassified by default; consultants classify between Session 1 and Session 2. The Phase 1 output document at end of Session 2 has zero unclassified processes (or any remaining unclassified are flagged as work-in-progress). A future scalar implementation-priority field (PI-011) attaches separately when Phase 2 Slice Planning content lands in v0.5+; this field carries methodology classification, that one carries implementation ranking. Cross-spec consistency check confirms process is the only methodology entity with a classification field in v0.4 (domain and entity have no analog).
context: Principle 3 of the evolved methodology says priority is established at the process level and inherited downward; mission-criticality is the consistent organizing principle. Phase 1 produces three priority buckets in its Prioritized Backbone output: Backbone (mission-critical), Supporting, Deferred. Between Session 1 (process names captured from client conversation) and the between-session drafting work that classifies them, processes exist in an unclassified state — the Phase 1 guide explicitly says 'do not yet propose mission-critical classification — that's between-session work' (line 237).
decision: Adopt process_classification as the field name (not process_priority — the user identified the methodology's three-bucket classification as conceptually distinct from a conventional scalar priority and pushed back on the priority terminology). Enum values: unclassified (default starter), mission_critical, supporting, deferred. Transitions: one-way out of unclassified (no regression to unclassified once classified); free bidirectional movement among the three classified values per Principle 7 ('decisions inside shipped iterations are locked; priority is not'). Server-side classification-transition validation per section 3.5.3 returns HTTP 422 on invalid transitions with {"error": "invalid_classification_transition", "from": ..., "to": ...}.
rationale: Field naming: the user observed that conventional priority semantics imply a scalar (Critical/High/Medium/Low) ranking, whereas mission-critical/supporting/deferred is a classification fused with scope decisions and role descriptions. Calling the field 'priority' would misrepresent its semantic. 'classification' is more accurate and matches the methodology's actual semantic (a methodology classification fused with engagement-scope information). Default unclassified reflects the Phase 1 cadence faithfully — Session 1 captures names without classifying; between-session work classifies; Session 2 verifies the classifications. Making classification required-on-create would either block Session 1 capture or force premature pattern-matching. One-way out of unclassified protects the classification work as a meaningful engagement event; demoting a classified process should change to deferred rather than regress to unclassified. Free movement among classified values supports Principle 7's explicit allowance for cross-iteration priority review.
title: process_classification field with four-value enum and unclassified default

alternatives_considered: Domain via references-entity edge with new vocab kind (rejected — references mechanism is for many-to-many, not many-to-one with settled cardinality). Process-to-process undirected (rejected — methodology language is directional and would create rendering ambiguity). Process-to-process named process_connects_to_process or process_feeds_process (rejected — hands_off_to matches the methodology guide's literal word). Pre-register process_touches_entity in v0.4 with no UI to create the edges (rejected — risks ossifying the wrong name when Phase 3 may need read/create/update/delete decomposition). Pre-register finer-grained process_reads_entity / process_creates_entity / process_updates_entity / process_deletes_entity in v0.4 (rejected — premature commitment without Phase 3 design work).
consequences: process table has process_domain_identifier FK column; processes always belong to exactly one live domain. process_hands_off_to_process registered in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS and _kinds_for_pair; Alembic migration extends refs.relationship_kind CHECK constraint. process_belongs_to_domain stays a conceptual relationship name only — referenced informationally but not in vocab. Process-to-entity vocab waits for v0.5+ work under PI-005. Cross-spec consistency check confirms no name collision between process_hands_off_to_process and entity_scopes_to_domain (clearly distinct).
context: Process is the most relational of the four methodology entity types. It belongs to one domain (many-to-one), connects to other process records through workflow handoffs (many-to-many), and is anticipated to touch entity records in v0.5+ once Phase 3 work demands. The mechanism for each connection (direct FK column vs references-entity edge) is decided per the spec guide section 3.3 case framework: direct FK when cardinality is settled and there's no edge metadata; references-entity edge when many-to-many or when metadata attaches to the edge.
decision: Three architectural sub-decisions: (1) Domain affiliation via direct FK column (process_domain_identifier on the processes table). Many-to-one cardinality is settled at the methodology level (a process belongs to exactly one domain — Phase 1's Prioritized Backbone groups processes by domain). No vocab.py registration because the references vocab governs the references table only and direct FK doesn't produce references rows. (2) Process-to-process handoffs via references-entity edge with new vocabulary kind process_hands_off_to_process. Directional (source = producer, target = consumer) reflecting Phase 1 guide section 4.1 language: 'Process A hands off to Process B if A produces records or state that B requires to do its work.' Many-to-many cardinality (each process can hand off to many and receive from many). Self-loops technically valid but unusual. Bidirectional handoffs (feedback loops) require two distinct edges (A → B and B → A); the references uniqueness constraint prevents duplicates of identical (source, target, kind) tuples. (3) Process-to-entity touches deferred entirely. No vocab registration in v0.4. The anticipated v0.5+ kind (process_touches_entity, possibly decomposed into read/create/update/delete variants) lists informationally in section 3.3.2 but is not added to REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS or _kinds_for_pair. Phase 1 does not formally produce this data; Phase 3 may need finer-grained kinds that a pre-committed coarse name would ossify.
rationale: Domain FK choice: cardinality is settled (per the methodology), edge has no metadata, lookup is trivial via FK. References-entity edge would be overkill and inconsistent with entity.md's many-to-many entity_scopes_to_domain only because the underlying cardinalities differ. Directional process-to-process: methodology language is inherently directional ('hands off'); Phase 1 output (section 7.3) names both directions distinctly per process ('handoffs to/from other backbone processes'). Undirected representation would force the consultant to track direction outside the system and force renderers to derive direction at display time. Cost of directional: feedback loops require two edges, but feedback loops are rare in Phase 1 backbones. Process-to-entity defer rationale: shipping a vocab name without Phase 3's actual semantic detail is a worse bet than waiting; PI-005 already tracks the work. Cost of defer: consultants who want to capture entity-touch data during Phase 1 fall back to freeform notes in process_notes, but Phase 1's official output does not require touches so the friction is small.
title: process relationship architecture: direct FK for domain, references-entity directional edge for handoffs, defer process-to-entity entirely

alternatives_considered: Bulk-create endpoint (rejected — not in scope for v0.4; case for it has not surfaced). Inline-handoff convenience endpoint (rejected — diverges from v2's decomposed-reference posture). Include Domain column in v0.4 master pane (rejected — would render DOM-NNN identifiers; same posture as entity.md). Collapse Hands-off-to and Receives-from into one references sub-section (rejected — loses directional information). Lock create-dialog handoff flow now (rejected — UI-layer decision better coordinated with parallel entity decision at v0.4-build planning).
consequences: process API surface is implementable without surprises against the v0.3 governance-entity patterns. UI is implementable as a ListDetailPanel subclass with the standard CRUD-dialog suite plus a ReferencesSection rendering bidirectional process_hands_off_to_process edges in two named sub-sections. Acceptance criteria #10 through #15 give the v0.4 build concrete UI tests. The Domain-column deferral creates one v0.5+ follow-on PI (parallel to PI-009 but for the Processes panel) if CBM redo signal warrants — not authored now to avoid premature PI proliferation. Create-dialog handoff flow open question gets resolved at v0.4-build planning, coordinated with the parallel entity affiliations question.
context: The process methodology entity needs a REST API surface, a desktop UI panel, and concrete acceptance criteria for v0.4 build. Defaults from domain.md and entity.md are honored unless deviation is methodology-driven; the spec guide section 3.5 (API), 3.6 (UI), and 3.7 (acceptance criteria) provide the template.
decision: Standard endpoint set: GET /processes (with ?include_deleted=true), GET /processes/{identifier}, POST /processes, PUT /processes/{identifier}, PATCH /processes/{identifier}, DELETE /processes/{identifier} (soft-delete), POST /processes/{identifier}/restore, GET /processes/next-identifier. No deviations. Server-side classification-transition validation per section 3.4.2; server-side domain-FK validation rejecting references to non-existent or soft-deleted domains. Decomposed reference handling for handoffs: separate POST /references calls with source_type=process, target_type=process, relationship_kind=process_hands_off_to_process. No inline-handoff convenience endpoint. UI: Methodology sidebar group position #3 (Domains, Entities, Processes, CRM Candidates — all four ship together in v0.4). Master pane columns Identifier / Name / Classification / Updated, sorted by Identifier ascending. Domain column deferred to v0.5+ paired with PI-007 short codes, mirroring entity.md's deferral posture (PI-009 tracks the entity-side column; if the CBM redo reveals the Processes panel needs an analogous column, a follow-on PI is authored at that time). Detail pane vertical layout with fields in section 3.2 order; ReferencesSection renders bidirectional process_hands_off_to_process references in separate Hands-off-to and Receives-from sub-sections. Create-dialog handoff flow (create-then-attach vs create-with-attach) left as a v0.4-build decision matching the parallel open question on entity affiliations. 15 testable acceptance criteria cover migration, validation, access-layer methods, REST endpoints, identifier helper, soft-delete/restore, sidebar position, master pane, detail pane, CRUD dialogs, file-watch, vocab registration, references round-trip, sample CBM Phase 1 backbone authoring.
rationale: No-deviation default endpoints minimize cross-spec complexity. Server-side validation (classification-transition, domain-FK) prevents UI-only enforcement bugs from corrupting engagement-scope state. Decomposed reference handling keeps the API consistent with v2's references-first discipline (DEC-006) and matches the entity.md / domain.md precedent. UI sidebar position #3 follows workstream order. Master pane Domain-column deferral mirrors entity.md (DEC-054) — without short codes the column renders DOM-NNN identifiers that don't tell a consultant anything at a glance, and the batched-join query is design work not yet validated against real-engagement signal. Detail pane separate Hands-off-to and Receives-from sub-sections honor the directional semantics of process_hands_off_to_process — collapsing them into a single 'Related processes' list would lose the direction information consultants need to read the backbone correctly. Create-dialog handoff flow open question matches entity.md's posture rather than locking the choice without v0.4-build coordination on parallel UX.
title: process API surface, UI defaults, and acceptance criteria for v0.4

alternatives_considered: CRMC (four letters — 'CRM Candidate'): rejected for adding a letter the methodology context already supplies; the kickoff and workstream plan both worked from CRM as the assumption and no disambiguation friction surfaced during the conversation. CC (two letters): rejected because the spec guide section 6 disallows prefixes under three letters, and collision risk would be substantial. CAND (four letters, 'candidate'): rejected as too generic — 'candidate' is the conceptual category shared with domain/entity candidate-statused records, not the entity name; the prefix should name what the entity IS, not its starter status.
consequences: crm_candidate.md section 3.1 documents the prefix and the rationale for affirming three letters rather than invoking the explicit deviation clause. No collision with existing prefixes (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC). Identifier-asymmetry helper endpoint (GET /crm_candidates/next-identifier) ships alongside the standard 8-endpoint set per DEC-043. Workstream-level: CRM completes the four prefixes (DOM, ENT, PROC, CRM); all four ship together in v0.4 under the new Methodology sidebar group.
context: The crm_candidate methodology entity needs an identifier prefix. Per domain.md section 3.1 (DEC-044), the soft-3-letter posture preserves flexibility for downstream methodology entity types to adopt 4-letter prefixes if the 3-letter form is ambiguous (process.md adopted PROC under this clause per DEC-055). The working assumption from the workstream plan (line 168) and from the schema-design-kickoff-crm_candidate.md document was CRM (three letters). Potential ambiguity worth considering: 'CRM' as a string also denotes 'the CRM software' broadly and 'CRM Builder' specifically.
decision: Adopt CRM as the identifier prefix for the crm_candidate methodology entity. Format CRM-NNN, zero-padded to 3 digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /crm_candidates/next-identifier per DEC-043. Affirms the soft-3-letter posture established by DEC-044 without invoking the explicit deviation clause.
rationale: CRM reads cleanly in the methodology context: a CRM-NNN identifier names a candidate product (the kind of thing the engagement might deploy to). The disambiguation against 'the CRM software' or 'CRM Builder' is supplied by usage context — in the v2 UI and methodology documents, identifier appearances read as 'CRM-001 (EspoCRM)' or similar, with the human-readable name supplying immediate disambiguation. Three letters also adheres to the v2 governance-entity norm (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA — all three letters), maintains consistency with the other three workstream prefixes where their 3-letter form is unambiguous (DOM, ENT), and avoids adding a letter the methodology context already supplies.
title: crm_candidate identifier prefix and format

alternatives_considered: Option B (Option A plus crm_candidate_vendor_url — free-text URL field, no enum cost, no Alembic CHECK constraint, no UI combo): considered for its low marginal cost and consultant-utility (saves searches when needing vendor docs). Rejected on the same minimum-viable principle: vendor_url is not Phase-1-output content per the phase outline; if CBM-redo surfaces utility, a one-column migration in v0.5+ adds it cheaply. Option C (full kickoff inventory: vendor_url + three enums): rejected as speculative — three CHECK-constrained enums in v0.4 commit the schema to particular taxonomy choices before any real-engagement signal has informed them; each enum requires Alembic constraint, access-layer validation, UI combo, and an additional acceptance criterion. Option D variant (vendor_url + hosting_type + license_type, dropping price_tier as the coarsest): not surfaced as a separate option but implicitly handled by the same minimum-viable principle applied to Option C.
consequences: crm_candidate.md section 3.2 documents the five fields with full validation rules. PI-012 (new) tracks all four deferred metadata fields for v0.5+ pending CBM-redo signal, with explicit trigger conditions for pulling the PI to v0.5 ahead of other v0.5 candidates. crm_candidate ships as the workstream's smallest schema (domain has seven fields, entity has six, process has seven; crm_candidate has five plus three timestamps for eight total columns), appropriately reflecting its position as the simplest of the four methodology entities. Phase 1 selection-criteria information lives in fit_reason prose in v0.4; analytics across engagements scanning for hosting / license / budget would parse text rather than query structured columns — acceptable cost at the small engagement count and pending CBM-redo signal.
context: The crm_candidate methodology entity hosts Phase 1 Initial CRM Candidate Set members per the evolved-methodology phase outline: 2-3 CRM products per engagement, proposed by CRM Builder and verified by client, persisting across iterations as the input to Phase 3 multi-deploy. The schema-design-kickoff-crm_candidate.md document named a working minimum (identifier, name, fit_reason, status) plus four optional metadata fields (vendor_url as free text, hosting_type as cloud/self_hosted/both enum, license_type as open_source/commercial/freemium enum, price_tier as free/paid enum). The minimum-viable v0.4 scope philosophy demands the thinnest schema that faithfully hosts Phase 1's output. The choice was which subset of the optional metadata fields makes the v0.4 cut.
decision: Adopt Option A — thinnest. Five substantive fields: crm_candidate_identifier (server-assigned, CRM-NNN format, unique); crm_candidate_name (required plain text, non-empty trimmed, case-insensitive engagement-global unique); crm_candidate_fit_reason (required plain text, non-empty trimmed, one-paragraph Phase 1 selection-criteria rationale); crm_candidate_notes (optional plain text, consultant scratchpad); crm_candidate_status (required enum, default 'active' per DEC-062). No structured classification metadata in v0.4. No storage-level length caps. Parent-prefix field naming inherited from DEC-046 (all fields including identifier and timestamps adopt the crm_candidate_ prefix). Inherited timestamps (crm_candidate_created_at, _updated_at, _deleted_at).
rationale: Phase 1 produces 2-3 candidates per engagement, a count too small for scannability gains from structured enum fields to outweigh their schema-and-UI-and-migration cost. The Phase 1 selection criteria named in the phase outline (hosting / license / budget / integrations / team-IT) all live in crm_candidate_fit_reason prose naturally — a one-paragraph rationale covers them without forcing a particular enum-shape. The v0.4-deferred adjacent artifacts (Phase 3 deployment logs, Phase 4 Comparison Artifact) are the natural home for structured comparison data, and they don't exist in v0.4. Extracting structured fields from prose later is mechanical (add columns via Alembic migration, optional NULL on existing rows); deciding the right enum values is easier with real CBM-redo signal in hand than with speculation now. Case-insensitive engagement-global name uniqueness mirrors DEC-045 / DEC-051 / DEC-056 cross-spec precedent.
title: crm_candidate field inventory and validation under minimum-viable v0.4 scope

alternatives_considered: Model B — three-status enum (active / selected / declined) with mid-engagement drop via soft-delete, preserving the cross-spec rejection-via-soft-delete principle unchanged. Considered and rejected on the methodology-dynamics-differ argument above. Database-level CHECK constraint enforcing singleton-selected (e.g., a partial unique index where status='selected'): rejected because v2's integrity discipline places constraints at the access layer per DEC-006; the access-layer pattern keeps the constraint logic in one place and consistent with existing entity-type behavior, and keeps the constraint relaxable without a schema migration. Allowing transitions out of terminal states (e.g., selected → active for selection-decision reversal): rejected as a status-edit responsibility too large for a single field; if needed in practice the v0.5+ mitigation is a dedicated reverse-Phase-5-outcome administrative path rather than relaxing the terminal-state rule. Propose-verify pre-state (e.g., 'candidate' starter analogous to domain / entity): rejected because Phase 1 candidate-set proposal is a lightweight gesture (the consultant names two or three products and the client agrees in the same conversation), unlike domain and entity proposals which involve substantive back-and-forth — the verification event IS the record-creation moment in practice.
consequences: crm_candidate.md section 3.4 documents the lifecycle at length, including sections 3.4.1 (status values table), 3.4.2 (transition semantics), 3.4.3 (singleton-selected constraint), 3.4.4 (deviation rationale for 'removed' status), 3.4.5 (rejection-via-soft-delete preserved for the authoring-error path), 3.4.6 (no archived status), and 3.4.7 (soft-delete semantics). The cross-spec consistency check at the v0.4-build-planning conversation now has two documented deviations to validate: process's no-status-field deviation (DEC-056, process.md section 3.4.1) and crm_candidate's removed-status deviation (this DEC, crm_candidate.md section 3.4.4). Both are well-justified by methodology semantics and small in scope. The singleton-selected access-layer validator extends the standard transition-validation pattern from domain / entity / process; implementation cost is small. UX rough edge acknowledged and logged as a CBM-redo-surfaceable concern: recovering from a fat-fingered transition into 'selected' requires the soft-delete + restore-elsewhere path (because terminal states have no transitions out). If lived signal surfaces friction, a v0.5+ mitigation either relaxes the terminal-state rule for the selected→active direction or introduces an administrative reversal path.
context: The crm_candidate methodology entity has an engagement-long lifecycle ending in a deliberate selection outcome at Phase 5 (the client picks one winning CRM from the candidate set). Mid-engagement, a candidate may also be pulled from the set based on lived deployment experience across iterations (team-IT capabilities don't match, vendor terms change, deployment friction reveals an unworkable fit). The cross-spec rejection-via-soft-delete principle established by DEC-047 and reaffirmed by DEC-052 says 'status values track engagement-scope lifecycle; soft-delete tracks existence-in-the-record.' For domain and entity, 'we proposed it and the client rejected it' is existence-in-the-record removal handled by soft-delete — the consultant got the proposal wrong. For crm_candidate, mid-engagement removal is consultant-driven adjustment based on lived deployment experience, not authoring-error rejection — a meaningfully different methodology gesture. The conversation evaluated whether to preserve the cross-spec principle (three-status enum: active / selected / declined, with mid-engagement drop via soft-delete) or to deviate (four-status enum including 'removed' as a first-class status value, with soft-delete reserved for the authoring-error path only).
decision: Adopt Model A: four-status enum (active / selected / declined / removed) with three terminal states. Default starter status 'active' (records exist in v2 only after CRM Builder's Phase 1 proposal has been verified by the client; no propose-verify pre-state). Transition map: active → {selected, declined, removed}; all three non-active states are terminal with no transitions out, returning HTTP 422 with invalid_status_transition on any out-transition attempt. Access-layer singleton-selected constraint: at most one live (non-soft-deleted) crm_candidate per engagement may hold status='selected'; enforced on POST (creating selected when one exists), PATCH/PUT (transitioning into selected), and POST /restore (restoring a selected record when another live selected exists), returning HTTP 422 with selected_candidate_already_exists and the existing record's identifier in the error body. Deviation from cross-spec rejection-via-soft-delete principle (DEC-047 / DEC-052): 'removed' is a first-class status value, not soft-delete. Soft-delete preserved for the authoring-error path only (record created in error — wrong product name, accidentally-added duplicate, etc.); the cross-spec principle survives intact for that path. Deviation rationale documented at length in crm_candidate.md section 3.4.4, following the deviation-rationale pattern that process.md set with its own no-status-field deviation in section 3.4.1 (DEC-056).
rationale: Three rationales. (1) For 'removed' as a first-class status rather than soft-delete: a mid-engagement-removed candidate was legitimately in the set, was lived-deployed-to during some iterations, and was correctly pulled before Phase 5 based on real deployment signal. That history carries methodology value — Phase 5 selection rationale may cite what was considered and rejected, governance decisions documenting the removal reference the candidate, retrospectives across engagements scan past candidate sets. Demoting such records to soft-deleted-and-hidden mis-frames legitimate methodology gestures as errors. The dynamics differ enough from domain / entity / process (where rejection is client-driven verification of a consultant proposal) to justify the deviation, and the deviation is small in scope (one extra enum value, one deviation-rationale subsection, no new infrastructure). (2) For terminal states with no transitions out: each non-active value represents a real engagement event (Phase 5 outcome or deliberate mid-engagement removal). Reversing such an event after the fact is a bigger methodology gesture than a status edit can faithfully represent; if revision is needed (e.g., the client changes their selected CRM after closure), a fresh governance decision record and explicit administrative action are the correct mechanism. (3) For the singleton-selected constraint at the access layer (not DB-level): Phase 5 picks one winner; multiple selected records would be ambiguous about which CRM is the engagement's chosen platform. Access-layer enforcement matches v2's existing soft-FK pattern (FKs validated at the access layer per DEC-006, not enforced by SQLite) and keeps the constraint relaxable in future schemas (e.g., a hypothetical multi-winner methodology variant would only need access-layer logic changes, not a schema migration).
title: crm_candidate lifecycle: four-status enum, three terminal states, singleton-selected constraint, removed-status deviation from rejection-via-soft-delete

alternatives_considered: Registering new vocabulary kinds with names like cited_by_decision (source=decision, target=crm_candidate) and discussed_in_session (source=crm_candidate or session, target=session or crm_candidate): rejected because the universal kinds already cover the semantics and the new kinds would either duplicate existing meaning (is_about does the work) or introduce ambiguous naming (which direction is 'discussed in'?). Pre-registering the v0.5+ kinds (deployment_log_targets_candidate, comparison_compares_candidates) in v0.4: rejected because their source entity types (deployment_log, comparison_artifact) don't exist in v0.4 — registering kinds for hypothetical source types creates dead vocab entries with no real validation semantics.
consequences: crm_candidate.md section 3.3 documents the relationship posture (3.3.1 outgoing — none, plus the ENTITY_TYPES expansion as the sole mechanical addition; 3.3.2 inbound anticipated via existing universal vocabulary plus informational v0.5+ table; 3.3.3 cross-spec naming convention adopted trivially with no new kinds registered; 3.3.4 hierarchy — not used). The v0.4 build's vocab.py change is one ENTITY_TYPES expansion (adding crm_candidate alongside domain, entity, process). The Alembic migration extends refs.source_type and refs.target_type CHECK constraints; one migration covers all four new entity types. The v0.3 references-create dialog adapts automatically. Cross-spec consistency check at v0.4-build-planning validates that the four specs' vocabulary additions do not conflict (visual inspection during this conversation confirmed clean: only entity.md and process.md register new kinds — entity_scopes_to_domain and process_hands_off_to_process respectively — and the names don't collide).
context: The crm_candidate methodology entity does not relate to domain, entity, or process — CRM candidates are metadata about WHERE the iteration deploys, not about WHAT the iteration models. The two sides of v0.4's methodology schema (the what side: domain / entity / process; the where side: crm_candidate) are intentionally orthogonal. The schema-design-kickoff-crm_candidate.md document anticipated that governance entities (decisions, sessions) would need to reference crm_candidate records (for Phase 5 selection-decision capture, session records of candidate-set discussions, audit trails of mid-engagement candidate-set adjustments) and asked whether new relationship-kind values would be needed — naming 'cited_by_decision' and 'discussed_in_session' as working terms. The vocab.py inspection during pre-flight confirmed that the existing universal references vocabulary (is_about and references, valid for any pair) and the existing decided_in kind (valid when target is session) already cover those semantics.
decision: No outgoing relationships from crm_candidate in v0.4. No new relationship-kind values registered in REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS. Single mechanical addition: crm_candidate added to ENTITY_TYPES in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/vocab.py, alongside the parallel additions of domain, entity, and process from this v0.4 release. The Alembic migration extends refs.source_type and refs.target_type CHECK constraints to admit all four new methodology entity types in a single coordinated migration. By virtue of being in ENTITY_TYPES, crm_candidate automatically participates in the universal kinds (is_about, references — valid for any pair per the _kinds_for_pair generic clause), in decided_in (when source is crm_candidate and target is session, per the target=session rule), and in supersedes (when both source and target are crm_candidate, per the source_type==target_type rule). Inbound governance-entity citations (decisions, sessions citing crm_candidate) work via the existing universal vocabulary without further additions. No spec changes to the v0.3 DEC-033 cascading reference-create dialog are required — it reads RELATIONSHIP_RULES at render time and adapts automatically once vocab.py is updated.
rationale: The existing universal references vocabulary in vocab.py is rich enough to cover the kickoff's anticipated use cases: 'cited_by_decision' is covered by is_about / references from a decision source (renders inbound on crm_candidate as 'decided_in DEC-NNN' equivalent meaning); 'discussed_in_session' is covered by is_about from a session source or decided_in to a session target. The kickoff's working names were imprecise; the correct names are the existing universal ones. Phase 5 selection-decision capture lives in a governance decision record (DEC-NNN) with is_about edges to the chosen crm_candidate; no new vocabulary is needed for that pattern. Adding redundant relationship kinds would proliferate vocabulary without adding methodology expressiveness — exactly the kind of vocabulary bloat the cross-spec principle '{source}_{verb}_{target}' naming (DEC-048) and the careful gate on REFERENCE_RELATIONSHIPS additions (DEC-006) exist to prevent. The v0.5+ anticipated kinds (deployment_log_targets_candidate from Phase 3 deployment-log artifacts, comparison_compares_candidates from Phase 4 Comparison Artifacts) are deferred along with the entity types that would source them (PI-004 territory: the deployment_log and comparison_artifact entity types are themselves deferred to v0.5+).
title: crm_candidate relationship posture, ENTITY_TYPES expansion, and use of existing universal references vocabulary

alternatives_considered: Database-level CHECK constraint or partial unique index enforcing singleton-selected (e.g., 'UNIQUE INDEX ON crm_candidates (status) WHERE status=selected AND deleted_at IS NULL'): rejected because v2's soft-FK and status-transition disciplines place integrity at the access layer per DEC-006; matching that pattern keeps the constraint logic uniform and consistent with existing entity-type behavior, and keeps the constraint relaxable without a schema migration in future methodology variants. Inline reference-attachment in create / edit dialog bodies (e.g., POST /crm_candidates with embedded references payload): rejected per the cross-spec decomposed reference handling pattern (DEC-053, DEC-058); crm_candidate has no outgoing references in v0.4 anyway, making the question moot in practice. Master-pane Domains-style column for status grouping or affiliation rendering: not applicable here (crm_candidate has no domain affiliation per the orthogonal-sides-of-methodology-schema posture); the corresponding question for crm_candidate is master-pane sort behavior for terminal-state records, deferred to v0.4-build as a small UX question.
consequences: crm_candidate.md sections 3.5 (API surface), 3.6 (UI considerations), 3.7 (twelve acceptance criteria) document the implementation requirements. v0.4-build-planning conversation translates the 12 acceptance criteria into specific test cases for the build slice covering crm_candidate, and decides the two deferred UI questions (master-pane sort behavior, delete-dialog clarifying-note wording) along with the parallel deferred questions from entity.md and process.md (create-dialog affiliation/handoff flows). The full v0.4 release ships all four methodology entity panels together as a single visible-to-user unit under the new Methodology sidebar group, completing the workstream's deliverables.
context: Per cross-spec conventions from DEC-049 / DEC-054 / DEC-059, methodology entity types ship with a standard 8-endpoint API surface (GET list, GET single, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, POST /restore, GET /next-identifier per DEC-043) and default ListDetailPanel UI under the Methodology sidebar group introduced by domain.md. The crm_candidate spec required confirming the standard set with no deviations, settling the singleton-selected enforcement details from DEC-062 at the API layer, drafting UI specifics for the new panel, and producing testable acceptance criteria.
decision: Standard 8-endpoint API surface with no deviations from cross-spec defaults. Server-side status-transition validation per crm_candidate.md section 3.5.3 returning HTTP 422 with invalid_status_transition error body. Singleton-selected enforcement at the access layer on POST (creating with status='selected' when another live selected exists), PATCH/PUT (transitioning into selected when another live selected exists), and POST /restore (restoring a selected record when another live selected exists), returning HTTP 422 with {"error": "selected_candidate_already_exists", "existing": "CRM-NNN"} per crm_candidate.md section 3.5.4. Default ListDetailPanel UI under the existing Methodology sidebar group at position #4 (completing the workstream-ordered set: Domains, Entities, Processes, CRM Candidates — all four ship together in v0.4). Master pane columns: identifier / name / status / updated_at; default sort by identifier ascending. Detail pane in section-3.2 order: identifier (read-only label), name (single-line editor), fit_reason (multi-line editor with placeholder), notes (multi-line editor collapsed under 'Internal notes' header), status (combo box restricting choices to valid successors of current status), ReferencesSection widget (renders inbound governance-entity citations; outgoing-references list empty in v0.4). Standard create / edit / delete dialogs; delete-dialog includes a clarifying note distinguishing soft-delete (authoring-error path) from status='removed' (legitimate mid-engagement drop). Twelve testable acceptance criteria covering schema migration, identifier format constraint, engagement-global case-insensitive name uniqueness, status enum and transition validation, singleton-selected enforcement, access-layer method signatures, REST endpoint round-trip, soft-delete/restore for the authoring-error path, ENTITY_TYPES registration and references-table CHECK constraint extension, sidebar entry placement, master pane columns and sort, and end-to-end CBM-redo Phase 1 + Phase 5 round-trip through the UI. Master-pane sort behavior for terminal-state records (interleave vs status-first) and exact delete-dialog clarifying-note wording deferred to v0.4-build as small UI questions, not architectural choices.
rationale: The cross-spec API and UI defaults are well-established by three predecessor specs (domain, entity, process); no methodology-specific need to deviate. Singleton-selected enforcement at the access layer matches v2's existing soft-FK and status-transition validation patterns (HTTP 422 with structured error body), keeping the constraint logic uniform across methodology entity types and consistent with existing entity-type behavior. The constraint applies on all three paths (POST, PATCH/PUT, restore) because each can introduce a second selected record into the live set — handling all three uniformly through the same validator simplifies implementation. The ReferencesSection widget existing in v0.3 renders inbound governance-entity citations for free once ENTITY_TYPES is updated; no panel-specific UI infrastructure is needed for the inbound-references case. Sidebar position #4 follows workstream ordering established by the four schema-design kickoffs and confirmed in each predecessor spec's section 3.6.1. Twelve acceptance criteria balances coverage (matching the predecessor counts: domain 14, entity 16, process 15, crm_candidate 12 — reflecting crm_candidate's simpler relationship posture and absence of new vocabulary kinds) against minimum-viable test scope (each criterion is testable in the v0.4 build, not in the schema-design conversation).
title: crm_candidate API surface, UI defaults, singleton-selected enforcement, and acceptance criteria for v0.4

alternatives_considered: (a) Follow the PRD literally with UUID PKs on catalog tables only - rejected: introduces a second PK idiom, complicates the change_log/refs/export machinery that assumes integer ids, and the methodology FK affordance does not actually require it. (b) Migrate the entire v2 stack to UUID PKs - rejected: far out of scope for catalog ingestion.
consequences: Catalog rows are addressed externally by catalog_id (entities) and catalog_id.attribute_name (attributes), never by integer PK - the JSON exports and REST/MCP surfaces already exclude internal ids. When the methodology entity schema lands, its primary_catalog_entity_id FK targets catalog_entity.id (INTEGER). If a future productization step needs globally-unique opaque ids, a uuid column can be added without disturbing the PK.
context: catalog-ingestion-PRD-v0.1.md section 4 specifies a UUID primary key on every catalog table, and section 3 lists 'UUID primary keys' as an integration affordance for the future methodology entity schema. The existing v2 storage system (charter, status, decisions, sessions, risks, planning_items, topics, refs, change_log) uses INTEGER autoincrement primary keys with a separate stable string 'identifier' column. The catalog-ingestion implementation plan section 3 explicitly instructs: 'if V2's actual layout differs, mirror the actual convention.'
decision: All 10 catalog tables use INTEGER autoincrement primary keys. catalog_entity carries the stable string identifier in its existing 'catalog_id' column (UNIQUE NOT NULL); catalog_attribute is addressed by (catalog_entity_id, name). The universal references table's target_id (already TEXT/String(64)) addresses catalog rows by catalog_id for entities and by '{catalog_id}.{name}' for attributes.
rationale: Consistency with the entire existing v2 storage stack outweighs literal adherence to the PRD's UUID spec. The PRD's stated affordance is a STABLE identifier that the methodology schema can FK to and that the universal-references vocabulary can target - the catalog_id text column provides exactly that, independent of PK type. A future methodology_entity table can carry a primary_catalog_entity_id FK to catalog_entity.id (INTEGER) just as cleanly as to a UUID. Mixing UUID PKs into a stack that is otherwise uniformly INTEGER would have created two ID idioms in one database for no functional gain.
title: Catalog tables use v2's INTEGER PK + catalog_id-TEXT convention, not the PRD's UUID-PK spec

alternatives_considered: (a) Build the plan's storage/ subtree - rejected: would have orphaned the catalog code in a layout no other v2 subsystem uses. (b) Add response Pydantic models with FastAPI response_model= enforcement - rejected: no other v2 router does this; the PRD's response contract is documented in the access-layer read docstrings instead. (c) Keep catalog access as a single flat module like the other repositories - rejected: too large; the read/write/exports split is genuinely clearer.
consequences: A reader familiar with any existing v2 entity (decisions, sessions, etc.) finds the catalog code in the expected places. The access/repositories/catalog/ package sets a precedent for future large subsystems (notably the methodology entity schema) to use a package rather than a flat module. If v2 later adopts response models wholesale, catalog will need a follow-up pass to add them.
context: The catalog-ingestion implementation plan section 3 assumed a 'storage/' subtree (crmbuilder_v2/storage/models/, storage/access/, storage/schemas/) and section 4 inventoried ~17 Pydantic models including a Read/Create/Update/Patch family with response models. V2's actual layout is flat: access/models.py (single file), access/repositories/<entity>.py, api/routers/, api/schemas.py (single file), mcp_server/tools.py. V2's existing routers return Envelope-wrapped plain dicts; there are no response Pydantic models anywhere in v2, and request models use the '*In' suffix.
decision: Catalog code mirrors v2's actual conventions: catalog models appended to access/models.py; access layer is a new access/repositories/catalog/ package (read.py / write.py / exports.py / __init__.py) - the only structural evolution, justified by catalog being materially larger than any single existing repository; api/routers/catalog.py; catalog schemas appended to api/schemas.py with the '*In' suffix; MCP tools appended to mcp_server/tools.py. Response shapes stay as plain dicts wrapped in the existing Envelope - no response Pydantic models. The catalog data migration and loader live under bootstrap/ (catalog_loader.py) since that is where v2 keeps migration-time content code; Alembic revisions live in the repo-level crmbuilder-v2/migrations/versions/.
rationale: The implementation plan explicitly authorised mirroring the actual convention where it differs from the plan's assumptions. A single consistent idiom across the v2 codebase is worth more than matching a plan written before the storage system's final shape was observed. The access/repositories/catalog/ package is the one deliberate departure from 'one flat module per entity' - catalog ships ~3,400 lines of access code, far past the point where a single module stays readable.
title: Catalog code mirrors v2's actual flat module layout; Pydantic models are request-only with dict responses

alternatives_considered: (a) Emit full nested before/after blobs - rejected for v0.1: large rows, awkward diffs, premature. (b) Emit a shallow entity-scalar-only diff - rejected: misleading, since most catalog edits touch nested data the shallow diff wouldn't show. (c) Build a catalog-aware structured audit format - deferred: better tackled alongside the methodology entity schema, which has the same nested-write shape and the same audit need.
consequences: Catalog edits are auditable via git history of the db-export/catalog/entities/*.json files, not via the change_log table. A future workstream - most naturally the methodology entity schema build, which shares the nested-write problem - can design a structured audit format and retrofit catalog emit() calls. ENTITY_TYPES and the CHECK constraint already admit the catalog types, so that retrofit needs no further migration.
context: Every existing v2 mutating repository call invokes change_log.emit() with a flat {before, after} dict pair. Catalog entities are deeply nested (an entity carries attributes, each with enum_values / synonyms / presence cells, plus entity-level systems / sources / synonyms / relationships / relationship presence). The change_log.entity_type CHECK constraint and the emit() guard both validate against ENTITY_TYPES.
decision: Catalog write methods (create/update/patch/delete on entities and attributes) do NOT emit change_log entries at v0.1. catalog_entity and catalog_attribute were still added to ENTITY_TYPES and to the change_log.entity_type CHECK constraint (migration 0005) so the door is open, but no emit() calls are wired into catalog write.py.
rationale: The existing emit() before/after contract is built for flat records. Snapshotting a full nested catalog entity into a single JSON blob on every write is possible but the resulting change_log rows would be large and awkward to diff, and the pattern wasn't designed for it. The per-entity JSON exports (DEC-008) already give a git-tracked, diffable history of every catalog change - that covers the practical audit need for v0.1.
title: Catalog writes do not emit change_log entries at v0.1

alternatives_considered: Forcing all four specs into the original spec-guide section 6 conventions (rejected — would have required either dropping `process` status entirely or adding a vestigial single-value field; would have required redesigning `crm_candidate` to use soft-delete for legitimate removal). Retroactive governance retrofit as part of v0.4 (rejected — substantial migration; carrying as PI-006).
consequences: The four MVS schemas may ship as-is. Spec-guide section 6 amended with v1.1 entry. Future methodology entity types added in v0.5+ inherit the new conventions. Governance entities retain their conventions. PI-006 tracks the retrofit decision for when (and if) v2's governance entities should be aligned to the methodology conventions.
context: The methodology-entity-schema-design workstream produced four schema specs across SES-012 through SES-015. Each spec independently passed its per-spec completeness gate. Spec-guide section 7.2 requires a cross-spec consistency check at the head of the v0.4-build-planning conversation before integration. The two cross-spec conventions established mid-workstream (parent-prefix field naming and source-first relationship-kind naming) were not pre-decided in the original spec guide and required reconciliation against the section 6 conventions table.
decision: The cross-spec consistency check across `domain.md`, `entity.md`, `process.md`, `crm_candidate.md` is accepted as passing per spec-guide section 7.2, with three documented deviations admitted as well-justified: (a) `process` has no `status` field — `process_classification` carries that role and a single-value status would be empty (DEC-055); (b) `crm_candidate` uses `removed` as a first-class terminal status rather than soft-delete because mid-engagement candidate drops are consultant-driven adjustments not authoring errors (DEC-062); (c) `process` uses the 4-letter `PROC` identifier prefix rather than the 3-letter `PRC` because `PRC` reads as a typo (DEC-056). Spec-guide section 6 is amended to capture the two new cross-spec conventions established by SES-012 and applied across SES-013/014/015 (parent-prefix field naming per DEC-046; source-first `{source}_{verb}_{target}` relationship-kind naming for methodology vocab per DEC-048), with scope explicitly forward-only for methodology entity types (governance entity types retain their pre-workstream conventions until and unless PI-006 retrofit lands).
rationale: The three deviations are each justified inline in their respective spec's section 3 by their authoring decisions. `process` having no status is not a missing field but a structural correctness — process classification IS process status. `crm_candidate.removed` is the cleaner semantic for a candidate that was real but is no longer in consideration. `PROC` over `PRC` is readability. The spec-guide section 6 amendment is forward-only because retroactively renaming governance entity fields would be a substantial migration with no current customer value; PI-006 tracks that retrofit decision separately.
title: v0.4 cross-spec consistency check accepted with three documented deviations; spec guide section 6 amended for forward-only methodology-entity conventions

alternatives_considered: All-in-one (rejected per context). Per-entity-only (rejected per context). Foundation + closeout only with all four entity panels in one slice (rejected — too much UI work in a single slice for clean testability).
consequences: Six slice prompts produced and committed. Build sequence A → B → C → D → E → F with linear dependencies (B–E depend on A; F depends on A–E). Each slice has its own commit and acceptance gate.
context: v0.4 introduces four new entity types with shared foundation work (vocab additions, CHECK extensions, sidebar group container, retrofit) and panel-specific work per type. Three plausible breakdowns: (a) all-in-one mega-slice (rejected — too risky and untestable mid-flight); (b) per-entity slices only with foundation folded into first entity slice (rejected — foundation work then sits on the critical path of one entity's slice rather than being independently verifiable); (c) hybrid foundation + per-entity + closeout (selected — mirrors v0.3, slice-by-slice testable, foundation independently verifiable).
decision: v0.4 is decomposed into six independently-testable slices mirroring v0.3's hybrid pattern: Slice A foundation (vocab, refs CHECK migration, sidebar group container, eight retrofitted next-identifier helpers, spec guide section 6 amendment verification), Slices B/C/D/E one entity panel each in workstream order (Domain, Entity, Process, CRM Candidate), Slice F mechanical closeout (version bump, README, regression pass, smoke).
rationale: The hybrid structure is precedented (v0.3 used it for the governance UI build) and produced clean tests. Per-entity panel slices have stable scopes given the four MVS schemas. Foundation as its own slice means slice B starts from a known-good vocab/migration state rather than wrestling with foundation issues during entity-specific work.
title: v0.4 slice breakdown — hybrid six-slice structure (foundation + four entity panels + closeout)

alternatives_considered: Option B (create-with-attach: include reference multi-select widgets in the New dialog) — rejected per rationale; documented as a clean v0.5 upgrade if CBM redo surfaces friction.
consequences: Slices C and D both implement create-then-attach uniformly. Domain FK on Process is a scalar required field IN the create dialog (record can't submit without it); references are NOT in the create dialog. PI candidate for create-with-attach upgrade noted in PRD risks section.
context: v0.3 established the cascading reference creation dialog. v0.4's entity types are the first to ship with outgoing references baked into the spec (entity affiliations, process handoffs). The question is whether the New dialog should also offer reference attachment, or whether attachment is purely a detail-pane operation.
decision: Both the Entities panel (Slice C) and the Processes panel (Slice D) implement reference-attachment as a two-stage operation. Stage 1: the New dialog creates the record only, with fields covering scalar attributes only — including required FK fields like `process_domain_identifier`, which are scalars not references. Stage 2: after the record exists, the user attaches references from the detail pane via the existing "Add reference" affordance, which opens v0.3's cascading vocab dialog with the source pre-populated. Option A (create-then-attach) chosen over Option B (create-with-attach: include reference multi-select widgets in the New dialog).
rationale: Option A reuses the existing v0.3 affordance verbatim with no new UI work and produces consistent flows across all four panels (Domain and CRM Candidate have no outgoing references; Entity and Process do, but their New dialogs match Domain/CRM Candidate's no-references-in-New shape). Option B would introduce a new multi-select widget pattern and would couple record creation to reference validation, which complicates error surfacing. Option A's cost is more clicks for the consultant; Option B's cost is more UI implementation and one more error-surface to test.
title: Coordinated create-then-attach reference-attachment flow for entity affiliations and process handoffs

alternatives_considered: Split into Slice A1 (foundation) + Slice A2 (retrofit) — rejected as unnecessary subdivision for a small, coherent change set.
consequences: Slice A scope is larger than slices B–E but still testable in one commit. All twelve next-identifier endpoints (4 new methodology + 8 retrofitted governance) ship together with one shared util.
context: SES-010 surfaced an asymmetry: v0.3 governance entity types lacked next-identifier helpers, requiring identifier-aware clients to compute prefixes themselves. DEC-043 settled to retrofit the eight types. The retrofit could land as its own slice (Slice A1) or fold into Slice A.
decision: The SES-010 identifier-asymmetry resolution per DEC-043 — retrofitting `GET /<entity>/next-identifier` helper endpoints onto the eight existing prefixed-identifier governance entity types — is folded into Slice A foundation rather than split into a separate slice.
rationale: The retrofit is small (one new helper util + eight endpoint additions + ~14 tests), shares the foundation surface with the new methodology entity types' helpers (all twelve helpers share the same shared util), and produces no user-facing UI surface. Folding into Slice A keeps slice count at six and gives Slice A a coherent "everything needed before the first entity panel ships" scope.
title: Slice A foundation scope includes the SES-010 next-identifier retrofit

alternatives_considered: Option B (status-grouped) — rejected per rationale; reserved as v0.5+ candidate gated on CBM-redo signal.
consequences: Slice E master pane sorts by identifier ascending. v0.5+ planning conversation will revisit if CBM redo Phase 1 surfaces visual friction.
context: `crm_candidate` has four statuses with three terminal states. In a typical CBM engagement, candidate counts are small (3–5 active candidates initially, possibly 1–2 in `selected` after evaluation, the rest `declined` or `removed`). Two sort options are reasonable: simple identifier-ascending (Option A — terminal states interleave with active by identifier), or status-grouped (Option B — terminal states sink below active).
decision: The `crm_candidate` master pane in slice E uses simple identifier-ascending sort as its default (Option A). Status-then-identifier ordering (Option B — `active` first, then `selected`, `declined`, `removed` interleaved with `active` by identifier within each status group) is reserved as a v0.5+ candidate gated on CBM-redo signal.
rationale: At small counts, identifier-ascending is fine and matches the master-pane convention used by Domain and Entity panels. Status-grouped becomes more useful once candidate counts grow past ~10 with multiple terminal-state records cluttering the view. v0.4 doesn't have CBM-redo evidence yet that the count will grow that far; Option A is the conservative ship.
title: crm_candidate master-pane sort — simple identifier-ascending in v0.4; status-then-identifier reserved for v0.5+

alternatives_considered: Including PI-006 in v0.4 (rejected — substantial migration with no current value). Including PI-008 in v0.4 (rejected — independent scope deserving its own planning). Splitting either into a separate workstream-parallel slice (rejected — adds branch complexity without v0.4 benefit).
consequences: Both PIs remain in the database tracked but deferred. v0.5+ planning revisits.
context: PI-006 and PI-008 were carried into the v0.4-build-planning conversation as candidate inclusions. Both are substantial. PI-006 touches eight existing governance entity tables plus their downstream surfaces. PI-008 introduces a new UI subsystem (folder watcher) with its own architectural questions (which folder, duplicate handling, watcher error surfacing).
decision: Both PI-006 (governance-entity retrofit to parent-prefix field-naming convention) and PI-008 (in-app inbox folder watcher for close-out JSON payloads) are confirmed deferred to v0.5+ and explicitly out of v0.4 scope.
rationale: v0.4 is already a substantial release scope (four new entity types end-to-end). Including either PI doubles the work without proportional value. PI-006 has no current customer-facing surface (governance entities work fine with their current conventions); the new methodology entity types ship with the new conventions regardless. PI-008 is independent of methodology entity work and warrants its own planning conversation. Both belong in v0.5+ planning if/when prioritized.
title: PI-006 (governance-entity parent-prefix retrofit) and PI-008 (in-app inbox folder watcher) both deferred to v0.5+ outside v0.4 scope

alternatives_considered: Renumber catalog ingestion's DEC-065/066/067 instead (rejected — those decisions have already been recorded with their numbers; rewriting database history is more disruptive than renumbering the not-yet-recorded v0.4 decisions). Defer v0.4 approval pending some other ID-allocation scheme (rejected — the renumbering is simple and the build prompts are otherwise ready).
consequences: v0.4 PRD status transitions from "Draft — pending approval" to "Approved". v0.4 build prompts unblocked. Slice F prompt updated to produce its own session record draft at slice close (to close the gap that per-slice session-record authoring was not previously inlined in slice prompts). PI-013/014/015 enter the database tracked at v0.5+.
context: The v0.4-build-planning conversation drafted its DEC numbers anticipating SES-016 and DEC-065/066/067 would be available. Between draft and approval, the catalog ingestion build executed and claimed those IDs. The collision required renumbering before the v0.4 PRD could close out as approved.
decision: v0.4 PRD `ui-PRD-v0.4.md` is approved with renumbering deltas applied: the anticipated SES-016 and DEC-065 through DEC-070 numbering shifts to SES-017 and DEC-068 through DEC-073 because the catalog ingestion build (SES-016, executed 05-14-26) consumed those IDs during the gap between v0.4-build-planning conversation and approval. Three new planning items added to PRD §2 Out of Scope: PI-013 Cross-Domain Service representation, PI-014 Catalog FK integration for methodology entities, PI-015 Methodology entity renderers — surfaced by the reconciliation conversation (SES-018) as previously-untracked v0.5+ work. PRD §11 (Decisions to Be Recorded) updated to reflect the renumbering and the additional PIs. Implementation plan and spec guide updated in lockstep. Build prompts B/C/D/E retain inline semantic DEC anchors at their drafted numbers (DEC-067, DEC-069) as documentation references; PRD §11 carries the authoritative renumbering table for any reader needing to resolve them. Slice F prompt and foundation prompt updated with operator-facing references corrected to the renumbered identifiers.
rationale: The collision is bookkeeping, not a substantive design issue. Renumbering preserves all decision content while satisfying the database's unique-identifier constraint. The PRD's renumbering note documents the shift transparently for future readers.
title: v0.4 PRD approval pass with renumbering deltas after catalog ingestion ID collision

alternatives_considered: Path B — Frankenstein bridge to v1's master.db. Rejected because the work cost is roughly equal to Path A and the path leaves bridge code that must be unwound when v2 eventually builds its own engagement type (which DEC-039's deferred PIs implicitly forecast). Path C — env-var override only, no UI, no v0.5 entity work. Rejected because it kicks the operational mechanism problem down the road without building the registry, the UI, or the migration; the env-var mechanism exists in code today and the gap is everything around it. Path A' — same as Path A but treated as a single-sprint focused build. Considered as a fallback if delay matters more than scope discipline; not the chosen posture because v0.5 is naturally bounded at one entity type plus routing infrastructure and a single-sprint frame doesn't materially compress that.
consequences: v0.5 inserts between v0.4 ship and any CBM activity. The paper-test conversation (DEC-077) and CBM redo Phase 1 both wait on v0.5 shipping plus a CBM engagement being created in v2. The v2.db dogfood instance migrates into the new engagement model — the 24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, 66 references, 222 change_log entries, 13 status versions, 2 charter versions, and 42 catalog entities all survive the migration into a CRMBUILDER engagement record. Identifier sequences become per-engagement: CBM's first session is SES-001 within the CBM engagement, not SES-025+ in a global sequence. v0.5 does not pick up the deferred methodology PIs (PI-003 persona, PI-004 field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process_step, PI-013 Cross-Domain Service, PI-014 catalog FK, PI-015 renderers, PI-016 router-vocab) — those remain v0.6+ candidates. The v2 charter's incremental-migration framing is reinforced rather than abandoned; engagement management is the next migration item from v1 in a planned sequence.
context: v0.4 shipped four MVS methodology entity types (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) into the v2 database, with the stated rationale of enabling CBM redo to use v2 as its system of record. v0.4 closeout exposed that those tables live in the same SQLite file (crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db) that hosts the v2-build's own governance (24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, etc.). Running CBM Phase 1 against the dogfood instance would co-mingle two unrelated bodies of data in one file with one identifier sequence. DEC-039's working answer to the multi-tenancy question — 'one v2 instance per engagement, separate SQLite, separate API port' — was framed as a finding rather than a designed feature; the operational mechanism (env-var-pointed alternate DB paths) existed in code but no second instance had been created, no naming convention existed, no UI for switching existed, no runbook existed. The v0.5-orientation conversation also surfaced that v1 already has multi-client management: automation/db/master_schema.py defines a Client master table; automation/ui/clients_tab.py ships a Clients tab; automation/db/client_schema.py defines a 31-table per-client methodology schema including Domain/Entity/Field/Persona/Process/ProcessStep/Requirement/Decision/AISession. The v2 charter (DEC-001 and surrounding) frames v2 as the next major iteration of CRMBuilder with v1 functionality migrating onto v2's foundation over time; engagement management is the natural next migration item.
decision: **v0.5 builds engagement management properly in v2 as the next migration item from v1.** v0.5 adds one new methodology entity type, `engagement` (or final name confirmed in Conversation 1), structured under MVS-thin scope per the v0.4 pattern: identifier + code (constrained to match v1's Client.code shape so the two systems can co-reference engagements by the same code) + name + purpose + status + audit columns. v0.5 also adds the supporting multi-engagement routing infrastructure: per-engagement SQLite databases, an active-engagement state mechanism, a bootstrap/discovery mechanism for enumerating engagements, an engagement-switcher mechanism in the desktop app, migration handling across engagements, and migration of the existing v2.db dogfood instance into the new engagement model. The workstream is structured as three conversations (this v0.5-orientation conversation; Conversation 1 combining multi-engagement architecture with engagement schema design; Conversation 2 producing v0.5 PRD and implementation plan); strawman four build slices follow. See PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/v0.5-engagement-management-workstream-plan.md for the full plan.
rationale: Equal cost weighs the bridge-to-v1 option down. The Frankenstein path (Path B in the conversation) was named and weighed against building proper engagement management in v2 (Path A). Both paths require comparable work: a registry mechanism, an ActiveEngagementContext analog, a switcher UI or env-var workflow, a launcher, schema-compat handling, tests. The work cost is roughly equivalent. Path A produces no bridge code requiring eventual unwinding; Path B produces bridge code with a known sunset date (when v2 eventually builds its own engagement type). With cost equal, the path without technical debt wins. The migration framing also matters: every deferred v2 PI (PI-003 persona, PI-004 field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec, PI-005 process_step) is implicitly a migration item from v1's per-client schema. Engagement management is in the same category. Building it the v2 way once, at v0.5, is consistent with the broader plan; bridging now and reimplementing later is not.
title: v0.5 release scope: add engagement management and multi-instance routing as v2-native capability, not a bridge to v1's Client master DB

alternatives_considered: Fifth deferral with the same CBM-redo-friction trigger. Rejected — the trigger has not fired in four cycles and cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe; the deferral is silent abandonment. Fifth deferral with a different trigger (e.g., post-Phase-1 review). Rejected for the same reason in different form — trigger conditions that cannot fire in the planning horizon don't constitute real deferral. v0.5-bundled — ship styling inside v0.5 rather than parallel. Rejected because engagement management and styling are independent capabilities that share no functional dependency; bundling mixes unrelated change in the README and complicates release notes. Sequential v0.5-then-styling — wait for v0.5 to ship before opening styling. Rejected because the work is independent enough that sequencing adds no clarity and parallel execution shortens the gap before CBM Phase 1 lands on a styled system. Retire PI-001 entirely — drop the styling work. Rejected — the work is real and the system needs visual coherence for Phase 1.
consequences: PI-001's open status persists but its meaning changes from 'deferred work waiting for a trigger' to 'active parallel workstream with its own plan.' Two Conversation 1's queue in fresh chats after this conversation closes — v0.5's and styling's — and can open in any order. The styling workstream has its own kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-conversation-1-kickoff.md and its own workstream plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md. Slice coordination at the engagement-panel coupling point is whichever-lands-first wins; the other rebases. Version-bundling decision deferred to the styling workstream's Conversation 2. PI-001's description is amended via PATCH to reflect reopening (see PI-001 in this apply).
context: PI-001 was created in DEC-024 at v0.1 close on the principle that v2's early releases should ship structural capability first and visual polish later. Deferred three more times: DEC-026 at v0.2 close, DEC-037 at v0.3 close, DEC-042 at v0.4 close. DEC-042 added an explicit trigger mechanism: PI-001 would open ahead of any other v0.5 candidate if CBM-redo Phase 1 — running against the four methodology panels v0.4 delivered — surfaced visual friction on any of those panels. The v0.5-orientation conversation surfaced that this trigger cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe: CBM redo Phase 1 waits on v0.5 (DEC-075) plus the paper-test (DEC-077). The four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire is an anti-pattern; PI-001 has effectively become a placeholder rather than a real signal. Two paths: defer a fifth time with the same trigger that has never worked, or reopen as parallel work.
decision: **Reopen PI-001 as a parallel workstream alongside v0.5, executing independently of v0.5 build with its own kickoff, design pass, build planning, and slices.** Two-conversation structure mirroring v0.5's shape but smaller: Conversation 1 produces the design pass (palette, typography scale, spacing scale, radius/border/elevation tokens, density target, visual decisions for major component classes — output at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md); Conversation 2 produces the styling PRD and slice build prompts.
