decision: Five slices: A — foundation infrastructure (meta DB, ActiveEngagementContext, two-database API wiring, lazy-migration helper, sidebar Engagements group container, refresh-service extension) PLUS dogfood migration combined in one slice; B — engagement schema (dataclass, status enum), access layer (eight standard repository methods with validation), and REST API (eight standard endpoints with envelope shape, JSON snapshot hook); C — engagement management panel UI (ListDetailPanel subclass, master/detail panes, CRUD dialogs, forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case, refresh-service registration); D — switching mechanism (top-strip widget, picker dropdown, 12-step ActivationWorker with question-6 amendment, activation overlay) plus single-gesture creation+activation flow (NewEngagementDialog extending slice C's EngagementCreateDialog with file creation and activation steps); E — closeout (version 0.5.0 bump, README release note, end-to-end integration smoke test, final regression pass, manual smoke verification). Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1 (foundation only) / A2 (dogfood migration) split is the documented fallback if the slice A prompt exceeds healthy size.
rationale: Five slices balance build cadence with acceptance-gate granularity. Combining foundation with dogfood migration in slice A is justified because foundation alone is a non-functional intermediate state (the meta DB exists but no engagement records and dogfood data is still at the old path; the desktop cannot operate against this state without falling back to the old single-DB path or failing); separating them would require either a non-functional intermediate slice or a hidden fallback path in slice A's code, both of which are worse than combining. Splitting slice B (schema+API) from slice C (panel UI) is justified because each is substantial on its own — B's eight REST endpoints with full validation logic plus the JSON snapshot hook is a complete subsystem; C's master/detail panel plus three CRUD dialogs plus the forbid-active-delete edge cases is a complete UI surface — and combining them produces an oversize prompt with mixed concerns. Keeping the switching mechanism (slice D) as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker is justified because the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled: the picker click triggers the activation worker, the activation overlay binds to worker progress signals, and the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step; splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice. The A1/A2 split fallback is documented but not pre-committed; the decision is made at slice A's prompt-drafting time if size signals warrant.
title: v0.5 slice breakdown: five slices with foundation+migration combined

alternatives_considered: Option B: sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group. Rejected because switching becomes a multi-click sequence (sidebar → engagement panel → row → 'Activate' action) when it should be a single-click affordance. Option C: modal-only switching invoked from the sidebar with no top-strip. Rejected because modal-only switching breaks the user's mental model — switching is a transient action, not a configuration dialog, and forcing a modal interrupt for every switch is friction without payoff. Option D: top-strip-only without a sidebar group. Rejected because management UI discoverability suffers; new users wouldn't know where to find the panel. Option E: place the Engagements group below the existing sidebar groups instead of above. Rejected because v2-install-level scope is structurally above per-engagement scope; placement below would suggest the opposite hierarchy.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's two UI-placement questions (Q1 and Q2 in the kickoff), settled together because the affordances are coupled. Q1: where does the switching affordance live? Q2: where does the management affordance live? The engagement entity is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata vs domain-scope methodology content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group introduced in v0.4. Switching is a frequent action requiring always-visible affordance; management is a less-frequent action requiring discoverable affordance. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (every user interaction with v0.5 touches one or both affordances) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (top-strip vs sidebar-only vs modal). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decision: Three coordinated affordances: (1) always-visible top-strip switching widget positioned above the sidebar entries inside the sidebar container; renders the active engagement's name with code in parentheses and a Lucide chevron-down caret; clicking opens the picker dropdown. (2) Picker dropdown anchored below the top-strip; live engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending; paused and archived rendered in color.neutral.500 and sorted to bottom within their bucket; active engagement marked with a Lucide check icon; footer item 'Manage engagements...' separated by hairline divider, opens the management panel. Empty state ('No engagement selected' top-strip; picker contains only the footer item) handled. (3) Engagements sidebar group positioned above Governance with one entry titled 'Engagements' that opens the management panel — same panel that the picker footer opens. The management panel is a ListDetailPanel subclass per the v0.4 pattern: master pane with five columns (Identifier, Code, Name, Status, Last Opened); detail pane with form fields per engagement.md §3.6.3; standard CRUD operations including the forbid-active-delete behavior per DEC-101.
rationale: The top-strip placement gives switching an always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function. Switching is potentially frequent (multiple times per work session if a consultant is working across multiple engagements); requiring a sidebar click would add friction. The Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for users who treat the sidebar as their primary navigation; without the entry, the management UI would be reachable only through the picker's footer item which is less discoverable. Dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity — both paths arrive at the same panel. The sidebar group's position above Governance reflects the v2-install-level scope distinction (engagement records are routing metadata across all engagements, structurally above the per-engagement governance and methodology content). Picker ordering rules (live first by last-opened-at descending; non-live at bottom; active marked) match user expectations for an MRU-style picker. The empty-state handling is required for the fresh-install case where the picker has nothing to select from.
title: Engagement UI affordance placement: top-strip switching widget plus Engagements sidebar group plus dual paths to management panel

alternatives_considered: Option B: two-gesture flow with explicit Create then Activate affordances. Rejected because every creator would have to choose 'now or later' twice (Create dialog and then a separate Activate action), which is friction without payoff for the dominant use case (create-then-immediately-work-in). Option C: single-gesture with all-or-nothing rollback (any failure rolls back to no-engagement-created state). Rejected because activation failure after both creates succeed is a realistic transient state (port-release timeout, MCP launch failure, etc.) where rolling back the engagement record forces re-data-entry; preserving the record with a retry-or-stay choice is better UX. Option D: single-gesture with implicit retry on activation failure. Rejected because hidden retry behavior produces opaque latency and obscures real failures from the user; explicit retry is better.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's engagement-creation flow question (Q3 in the kickoff). Creating a new engagement requires three operations: POST /engagements (creates meta DB row), desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head, desktop initiates 12-step activation. The question is whether to expose these as a single user gesture (one click triggers all three) or as separate gestures (user clicks 'Create', then later clicks 'Activate'). The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (creation is a recurring action; the choice shapes every engagement-creation user experience) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (single-gesture vs two-gesture). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decision: Single-gesture creation+activation: the New Engagement dialog runs all three operations behind one user click on Submit. Three-label progress indicator advances through 'Creating engagement record...' → 'Initializing database...' → 'Switching to <name>...' Each label transitions to a Lucide check icon on success or a Lucide circle-x in color.danger.default on failure. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately: POST failure stays in the dialog with inline error per the v0.3 validation-error pattern; file-creation failure DELETEs the meta DB row to roll back; activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' and 'Stay in <previous engagement>' affordances and the engagement record persists. The slice-C EngagementCreateDialog (which creates the meta DB row only) remains in the codebase as a parent class for NewEngagementDialog; the slice-D NewEngagementDialog is the primary path for engagement creation after slice D lands, but EngagementCreateDialog is still available for scripted use cases (e.g., creating a record without activation for testing or recovery scenarios).
rationale: Single-gesture creation matches the user's mental model: creating an engagement implies wanting to work in it; forcing a separate 'now activate it' gesture adds friction without payoff. The three-label progress indicator gives a coherent narrative for the few-second sequence; the labels advance fast enough to read as confirmation of progress rather than as a slow operation. The graceful failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state (engagement record and file exist but activation failed) cleanly without silent rollback; the user explicitly chooses whether to retry or to stay in the previous engagement. The slice-C dialog remaining in the codebase as a parent class enables the slice-D extension via inheritance rather than duplication, and supports edge-case use through direct invocation.
title: Single-gesture engagement creation+activation flow with graceful inline failure recovery

alternatives_considered: Option B: accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. Rejected because the auto-switch target is ambiguous (most-recent? alphabetical? what if there's only one other? what if it's soft-deleted?); making the user choose via the picker is cleaner. Option C: hard error with no redirect (just refuse). Rejected because the message would leave the user wondering what to do next; the redirect affordance is small additional cost for substantial UX gain.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's active-engagement-soft-delete question (Q4 in the kickoff). When the user attempts to soft-delete the engagement that is currently active, two postures are possible: (a) refuse the operation and redirect to switching first, or (b) accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (the operation is uncommon by use-case design) AND only one option is genuinely defensible (refusing is cleaner; auto-switch introduces choosing-which-engagement-to-switch-to as a hidden decision). Decided as decide-and-announce.
decision: The engagement panel's Delete dialog detects whether the target row is the currently-active engagement (via comparison against ActiveEngagementContext.engagement_identifier). When active, the standard edge-text confirmation field is replaced with an inline message: '<engagement_name> is currently active. Switch to a different engagement first, then soft-delete this one.' The Delete button is replaced with a 'Switch engagement' button that opens the picker dropdown. Last-engagement edge case (the active engagement is the only one on the install): message changes to '<engagement_name> is the only engagement on this install. Create another engagement before soft-deleting this one.' The button changes to 'Create engagement' opening the New Engagement dialog (single-gesture variant in slice D, slice-C Create dialog before slice D lands). The next-launch drift-recovery path in engagement.md §3.4.5 remains as a safety net for cross-restart desync (e.g., user soft-deletes the active engagement via direct API call or external script, then restarts the desktop; current_engagement.json points at a soft-deleted engagement; in-memory context starts empty; picker prompts for selection).
rationale: Refusing the operation is cleaner than auto-switch: the user explicitly chooses which engagement to switch to (via the picker) rather than the system silently picking 'next most recent' or similar. Inline redirect (replacing the Delete button with 'Switch engagement') keeps the user in the same UI context, avoiding modal-stacking. The last-engagement edge case wording handles the corner case where switching isn't possible, redirecting to creation. The drift-recovery safety net handles out-of-band deletion that bypasses the dialog. The operation is uncommon by use-case design (consultants don't typically delete the engagement they're working in), so the additional friction of refuse-and-redirect is acceptable.
title: Forbid soft-delete on the currently-active engagement with inline redirect to switch first

alternatives_considered: Option B: pre-fill with a computed path like ~/Documents/crmbuilder/{engagement_code}/db-export/. Rejected because the path would be wrong for the dominant use case (client-repo-based) and right for an edge case (consultant-local-only). Option C: pre-fill with an absolute path computed from a configuration setting. Rejected because v0.5 has no such configuration setting and adding one for a default isn't justified.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's export-dir-default question (Q5 in the kickoff). The engagement_export_dir field on the engagement record is nullable per engagement.md §3.2; when set, it points at an absolute filesystem path where the engagement's JSON snapshots are auto-written. The New Engagement dialog needs to handle the default state: pre-fill with a computed path (e.g., a subdirectory of the engagement-code-named home directory)? Leave blank? The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (this is a configuration default, not a behavior change) AND only one option fits the Q8-of-Conversation-1 framing where null disables auto-export. Decided as decide-and-announce.
decision: The New Engagement dialog's export-dir field is empty by default with placeholder text 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export.' and tooltip 'Where this engagement's JSON snapshots will be written. Recommend a path inside the client repo so exports travel with the engagement documents.' Null is a first-class value (disables auto-export; the access-layer validates this case in slice B and the activation-time export check in slice D logs a warning if null is the value). The dogfood migration in slice A explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time); the migration's behavior is divergent from the dialog default because the dogfood engagement has a definite and computable export location while user-created engagements typically need explicit configuration of where exports should land.
rationale: Null default avoids guessing what the user wants (any pre-filled path would either need to be a sensible default for most use cases or would mislead users into accepting a value they hadn't actually chosen). The placeholder text makes the optionality explicit. The tooltip provides discoverability for the recommended pattern (path inside the client repo) without forcing it. The CRMBUILDER divergence from the dialog default is justified because the dogfood has a known export location (engine-repo db-export/) that the migration knows about; user-created engagements don't have an analogous always-correct default.
title: Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagement records

alternatives_considered: Option B: no JSON export for the meta DB; engagement panel refreshes via explicit API polling or via signals fired by access-layer writes only. Rejected because it breaks uniformity with the v0.3+ pattern and creates a special-case refresh mechanism for one panel. Option C: bundle the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/ as a single engagements.json at the parent level. Rejected because the dogfood's db-export/ is scoped to CRMBUILDER's content; mixing in install-level metadata produces a confusing artifact and a coupling between the dogfood and the meta DB that doesn't otherwise exist. Option D: place the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely. Rejected because doing so departs from the established v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern without justification.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's meta DB export location question (Q10 in the kickoff, tightly coupled to Q7's engagement panel refresh trigger). The meta DB hosts the engagements registry table; v0.3's JSON-snapshot pattern (DEC-022, DEC-008) makes writes regenerate snapshot files under db-export/ that panels file-watch for refresh. The question is whether the meta DB participates in this pattern and where its snapshot lands. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is moderate (the snapshot location affects how the engagement panel refreshes and where the artifact lives in the repo) AND there are two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (bundled into the dogfood's db-export/ vs separate location). Decided as an operational refinement.
decision: Meta DB has its own JSON export at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json. The db-export/meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/ (which are sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json, etc. — the CRMBUILDER engagement's content). Writes to the meta DB regenerate engagements.json via the standard access-layer hook synchronously within the same transaction. The path is fixed at the engine-repo level (not configurable per engagement, because the meta DB is install-scope routing metadata, not engagement-scope content). The engagement panel registers with the refresh service and refreshes on file change per the standard v0.3+ pattern.
rationale: Uniformity with the v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern: the meta DB participates in the same render-on-write discipline as per-engagement DBs. The meta/ subdirectory makes the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) — a future reader of the db-export/ directory tree can distinguish 'this is CRMBUILDER's content' from 'this is install-level engagement registry' without reading the file contents. Bundling the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/engagements.json is conceptually wrong because the dogfood's per-engagement DB doesn't contain engagement records; engagement records live in the meta DB; mixing them produces a confusing artifact. Placing the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely (e.g., at crmbuilder-v2/data/exports/) would require a separate file-watch pattern outside the v0.3+ convention.
title: Meta DB JSON exports at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json

alternatives_considered: Alternative scope shapes were not explicitly evaluated as alternatives — the scope was iteratively refined during the conversation through the ten settled questions and the consequential-decision presentations. The PRD shape (13 sections vs the kickoff's suggested 8-section shape) was chosen to match v0.4's shape per the kickoff's 'Shape follows ui-PRD-v0.4.md' direction.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's PRD approval gate (Q12 implicit in the kickoff's deliverables list). The PRD at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md and the companion implementation plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md were drafted in this conversation and presented for review. The PRD opens 'Draft — pending approval'; this decision records the transition to Approved.
decision: v0.5 UI PRD v0.5 approved: 10 in-scope items (multi-engagement routing infrastructure; dogfood migration; engagement entity type with eight standard endpoints; 12-step activation sequence with question-6 amendment; Engagements sidebar group and management panel; switching affordance with top-strip and picker; single-gesture engagement creation; forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case; meta DB JSON exports with file-watch refresh; About-dialog version bump and README release note), 16 out-of-scope items (multi-tenant API+MCP per PI-017; engagement-level access control; v1 Client import; cross-engagement queries; optional engagement_db_path override; engagement rename; hard delete with file removal; additional engagement-level fields; User Process Guide v0.2; auto-switch on active-engagement soft-delete; right-click 'Activate' on engagement panel; full styling design pass execution; methodology entity types beyond v0.4's four; methodology entity renderers; catalog FK integration; v1 NOT_SUPPORTED reimplementation workstreams), five-slice plan (A foundation+migration combined; B engagement schema+access+REST; C panel UI; D switching+single-gesture; E closeout), 10 acceptance criteria for slice E closeout plus the 13 architecture-level and 12 entity-level acceptance criteria distributed across slices A-D. Implementation plan companion approved at the same time. Five slice build prompts approved at the same time. PRD §9 coordination section rewritten at closeout to reflect the styling Conversation 2 v0.6 PRD that landed mid-conversation: v0.5 ships first per DEC-095's separate-release sequencing; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel and all other surfaces as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C. PRD transitions from Draft to Approved.
rationale: The PRD and implementation plan and five slice prompts together cover everything needed for v0.5 execution: requirements (PRD), build sequencing (implementation plan), step-by-step execution guidance (slice prompts). The release scope matches the workstream-plan and Conversation 1's architectural foundation; the slice plan optimizes for build cadence while preserving non-trivial acceptance gates; the deferred items have justified rationales (cited PIs, future versions, scope discipline). The PRD's 11 risks and mitigations and 5 open questions are appropriate for a build-time-tunable release. The §9 rewrite captures the actual coordination posture with v0.6 (which crystallized mid-conversation when the styling Conversation 2 deliverables landed on origin/main) rather than the pre-clarification posture that the original draft assumed.
title: v0.5 UI PRD approval — release scope and slice plan accepted
